- As individuals, there are about 3 things that we should do flawlessly on any given day, week, month, year. We should be OK with flaws in the remaining things. By choosing those 3 things wisely, we can cover the bulk of what it takes to be really successful.
- For teams, that number is about 5 things. And for companies, about 7 things.
author: Shreyas Doshi
commentary:
This is another one of those deceptively simple but hard to follow pieces of advice. I have personally benefited by focusing on only 3 goals in a month. I have been maintaining a monthly goals log for over 6 months and I have rarely come close to 60% achievement of what goals I initially set out to execute.

![](https://prod-files-secure.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/593c798c-00bb-48f4-8f8a-7c73d755e6bd/dbf48090-8362-46e4-97cd-d91487c0bf65/Untitled.png?X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Content-Sha256=UNSIGNED-PAYLOAD&X-Amz-Credential=ASIAZI2LB466W5O5CFDE%2F20260607%2Fus-west-2%2Fs3%2Faws4_request&X-Amz-Date=20260607T162945Z&X-Amz-Expires=3600&X-Amz-Security-Token=IQoJb3JpZ2luX2VjENj%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2FwEaCXVzLXdlc3QtMiJHMEUCIQDvTf9BrwVwhlLmNiqEUqZu0uiIb5ev%2B65aZmMqydJQfgIgWjeM3F%2F3qCPgOssF7MuP%2FPAFg9Z3oZQ3%2F0SmGqJioe8qiAQIof%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2FARAAGgw2Mzc0MjMxODM4MDUiDPwidutJL2Lmz1KIoCrcA4HqZydC9eVk49HpKzkakXAJYIpqO8xffr3MKXhQ%2FCDAc5mWA2lYBQ8Coa77fNc9l6C2UcoXYx%2BDCg%2BpfiV7u%2FjUSgbDXmmJQjyPisRp4VlKSPE8qoQMULEiDumB7HUvKCIudeafErXwImC9p8J5SFitpWQg60l2bJqRYaaPVbc9qMwGjirylXfqrXJ%2FZ%2BK71dZgHqyyGM%2BTrSPtcAP0GoM7JU%2BRilhyoixPzdEwQVvKGNOU3hApJewJp3u2vyayHg8oYqHOyNwCqXPEHzeAObFCtqpkQi6bV1nCN4myh2RXJb8JQNls2Y97PYfWjISNvljSdY8ezvsyZqM5kHvoUOHAHWWwzxvbDs284ZuVqhNSU%2BV0x9dedhAsDRkm0FsENokKBAC3V%2FRW2DWaSbz8%2FuDK%2FmUeWR3UHPv8OhqI6a7oCxdadjoKZ0UCyPySpQ%2FByDDtZfHI%2FfzqMQ8KzAGqI3jcON3MZMOktfepafXWlj9DjRS0bgYFf5tBjwqtrqjqfHgypupzdLGOphkxy6LehYOup%2Ff7%2Bxq%2BjcJo6UpbTruFhUoR3%2FqLRF7yUrqlwXUXQGv0uBWjoYYGW8LyivOj3onN%2BYtiAsJjSjw88Gr7dqMKqYfxTGmig8e9JPKLMNGultEGOqUBAIQOh2rM%2BNwpmOMWbkjNrPqPNjwueO4mldQHarO1PyIdlsq4ewQX9bGCCEeeDlu%2Bqgsi2zPY4nyHewumT0FWXj2eUY%2FXhyiVLR1zq%2FiGRw6PFa0u6Wez2OsJTlaHfgms7u%2Fs8tyziAkJRUs6X9QiJ8yz0tPnOi%2BfB9B5d%2BqGj6YIe0PqhkPpMsN6wCasXUjXmRxaH67mIm1khnbeP86f7XqmBSQ2&X-Amz-Signature=55808743bd2b42b0a2cb146fa20b6cc8cd77a91410b9d491b08955f5f2a8b052&X-Amz-SignedHeaders=host&x-amz-checksum-mode=ENABLED&x-id=GetObject)
summary: Do ~3 things flawlessly as an individual, ~5 as a team, ~7 as a company; accept flaws in the rest.
when_to_use: When you need to cap the number of things you commit to doing flawlessly so the vital few drive success.
when_not_to_use: When work genuinely requires juggling many concurrent priorities or the count of critical items is dictated externally.

name: Bullet Journal (Bujo)
body_md:
- The Bullet Journal (Bujo) method is a flexible and customizable analog system for organizing tasks, events, and notes. It combines elements of a planner, to-do list, and journal.
- Bujo encourages the use of rapid logging, which involves using simple bullet points to quickly jot down tasks, events, and notes. Employ different symbols or bullets to categorize entries. For example, use a dot for tasks, a circle for events, and a dash for notes.
- Every entry is categorized into three broad themes with a specific bullet type for each
	- Tasks - Action items
	- Events - Meetings, Conferences etc.
	- Notes - Takeways, Highlights etc.
- Some key practices and components of Bujo are highlighted below
	- **Monthly Log** for a monthly overview to record important events, tasks, and goals for the month ahead.
	- **Daily Log** to organize your day. Write down tasks, events, and notes, and mark them as completed or migrated as needed.
	- **Migration**: Regularly review your entries and migrate unfinished tasks or uncompleted notes to a new day or future log.
	- **Future Log** to note down upcoming events, deadlines, or tasks for the coming months.
	- **Collections** are specialized pages for specific topics or projects. These can be anything from a list of books to read to a travel itinerary.
	- **Index** page or section to keep track of the content and page numbers in your bullet journal
	- **Reflection**: Take time to reflect on your progress, evaluate completed tasks, and identify areas for improvement.
- The Bullet Journal method allows for flexibility and personalization, allowing you to adapt the system to your needs and preferences. It provides a structured yet adaptable approach to organizing and tracking your tasks, events, and ideas in a single notebook.
author: Ryder Carrol
commentary: BuJo is not a widely cited or popular philosophy in my networks. I tried using BuJo for over a year. I enjoyed the practice of writing weekly, monthly, and daily logs. It was also helpful in retrospect when I was trying to analyze where I spent time. I especially enjoyed how flexible it is and the shorthand writing style which I use for writing interview notes. Its focus on using a physical medium made it a challenge to persist long term.
summary: An analog rapid-logging system using bullets and migration to organize tasks, events, and notes in a single notebook.
when_to_use: When you want a flexible analog system to rapidly log and migrate tasks, events, and notes in one notebook.
when_not_to_use: When you need digital sync, reminders, or sharing, or won't sustain a daily handwriting habit.

name: Eisenhower Matrix
body_md:
- Categorize tasks/initiatives into two parameters: urgency and importance to create a 2x2 matrix.
	- Urgent tasks require immediate attention and must be done now.
	- Important tasks may not require immediate attention, but they help achieve your long-term goals.
- **Urgent and Important**: These tasks require immediate attention and are crucial for your long-term goals. They are time-sensitive and have high importance. Examples include launch planning, production bugs, etc.
- **Not Urgent but Important**: These tasks are essential for your long-term goals but do not have an immediate deadline or sense of urgency. Examples include product strategy narrative, roadmap, etc.
- **Urgent but Not Important**: Tasks in this quadrant are time-sensitive, but they do not significantly contribute to your long-term objectives. They are often distractions or interruptions, such as unnecessary meetings, non-essential emails, or minor tasks that can be delegated or eliminated.
- **Not Urgent and Not Important**: This quadrant includes time-wasting activities and distractions that do not contribute to your goals.
author: Dwight D. Eisenhower
summary: A 2x2 of urgency vs. importance that sorts tasks into do, schedule, delegate, or eliminate.
when_to_use: When a backlog mixes urgent and important work and you need to decide what to do, schedule, delegate, or drop.
when_not_to_use: When everything is genuinely both urgent and important, or when urgency/importance can't be meaningfully distinguished.

name: Getting Things Done (GTD)
body_md:
- The GTD method is based on a straightforward idea: the more thoughts you have going on in your head, the more difficult it becomes to figure out what you should focus on. 
- This system helps you to clear your mind by transferring all your ideas into an external system and then arranging them in a way that lets you concentrate on the most important tasks at the appropriate times.
- The following is a high level summary of the GTD workflow
- The GTD method is made up of five simple practices to systematize the clutter in your brain and get things done:
	- **Capture.** Collect what has your attention
	- **Clarify.** Process what it means
	- **Organize.** Put it where it belongs
	- **Reflect.** Review frequently
	- **Engage.** Simply do.
- High level principles
	- Every open loop must be in your capture system and out of your head
	- You must have as few capturing buckets as you can get by with
	- You must empty them regularly
	- If an action will take less than two minutes, it should be done at the moment it is defined.
author: David Allan
summary: A five-step workflow (Capture, Clarify, Organize, Reflect, Engage) that offloads open loops from your head into a trusted system.
when_to_use: When mental clutter and open loops make it hard to focus and you need a trusted external system to capture and process them.
when_not_to_use: When your workload is small enough to hold in your head, or you won't commit to regular capture and review.

name: LNO Framework
body_md:
- All tasks are not created equal. Categorize your tasks as leverage, neutral and overhead depending on the return you expect to get from the effort invested in completing them
- **Leverage tasks** are your most important (may not be immediate) tasks where you expect to get 10x or more value for your effort by doing a great job.
- **Neutral tasks** are where you get as much or a little more from the effort you put in. Aim to do a good job and no better
- **Overhead tasks** are where the returns are much lower than the effort you put in. Aim to do an ok job or a bad job (if you are a perfectionist & have high standards)
author: Shreyas Doshi
summary: Sort tasks into Leverage, Neutral, and Overhead and match effort to expected return instead of treating all tasks equally.
when_to_use: When triaging a task list and deciding how much effort each task deserves based on expected return.
when_not_to_use: When tasks are genuinely uniform in value or the work is purely mechanical with no room to vary effort.

name: Maker Vs. Manager Schedule
body_md:
- There are two types of schedules: the manager's schedule and the maker's schedule. 
	- In the **manager’s schedule** (usually senior leaders and team leads), **a day is divided into small time blocks according to the calendar**. It is easy to schedule meetings by finding available time slots. Managers rely on their calendar to determine their next task, minimizing the need to plan ahead. To effectively focus on a task, managers must avoid distractions. While managers do not require deep focus, they need the ability to make quick and informed decisions.
	- On the other hand, **makers need long, uninterrupted periods of time known as the maker’s schedule** to make progress on complex tasks and take on new challenges. These uninterrupted blocks of time are crucial for tackling difficult problems. Even a single meeting can disrupt their flow and make it harder to resume their work, as it requires them to switch contexts.
- It is common for a person's job to involve both maker and manager duties. It is important to distinguish between maker and manager schedules because doing maker work on a manager schedule, or vice versa, can lead to problems.
- It is also important to be aware of the schedule that the people around us are on, in order to allow them to do their best work. Managers and makers rely on each other, but some managers can hinder productivity when they fail to recognize the difference in schedules.
author: Paul Graham
commentary:
This essay and the concept of [Deep Work from Cal Newport](https://www.amazon.com/Deep-Work-Cal-Newport-audiobook/dp/B01CYKUC9C) have greatly influenced how I approach allocating time for creative work. Previously, I would attempt to find an hour each day to slowly chip away at leverage tasks without disrupting my day-to-day work. However, I eventually realized that setting aside a continuous, uninterrupted block of time (3-4 hours) allowed me to accomplish much more than small, daily increments. It typically took me an hour to fully immerse myself in a problem, only to have to switch to something else.

As PMs we are bombarded with meetings and request for our time. One effective tactic that worked for me was to schedule weekly recurring chunks of focus time on my calendar. It was set-up to automatically decline requests for any meeting telling people that I was on focus time. People were annoyed initially but over time they got used to it.
summary: Distinguishes the meeting-sliced manager's schedule from the maker's need for long uninterrupted focus blocks.
when_to_use: When deep creative work keeps getting fragmented by meetings and you need to protect uninterrupted maker time.
when_not_to_use: When your role is purely reactive coordination with no deep-focus work, or you have no control over your calendar.

name: ROI vs. Opportunity Cost Mindset
body_md:
- Most people implicitly use a maximizing Return on Investment (ROI) heuristic when selecting tasks to perform. However, when in a high leverage role, such as product leadership, it is advisable to shift to a minimizing Opportunity Cost mindset.
- **ROI**
	$$
	ROI = \frac{Value Created - Cost ofYourTime}{CostofYourTime}
	$$
	- To increase ROI you can either increase value created (work on high value things) or decrease cost (work on things that take less time)
	- When people have an ROI mindset, they naturally gravitate towards decreasing cost. A task that has a tiny cost will have a very high ROI even if it creates low value. 
	- In a high leverage role, it’s easy to find 100s of low cost tasks, but it’s hard to discern things that create massive value, and equally hard to overcome the inertia to take those things on.
	- With an ROI mindset, we occupy our time with quick, incremental wins, and we feel good about our “productivity”
- **Opportunity Cost**
	$$
	Opportunity Cost = ValueofOptimalSolution - ValueofChosenOption
	$$
	- With a minimizing opportunity cost mindset, we start to think about high value alternatives that we wouldn’t have otherwise considered with an ROI mindset
- Adopting an ROI mindset can result in small, incremental actions (like Brownian motion) that lack cohesion. On the other hand, an Opportunity Cost mindset enables you to move with more intent, making progress by miles instead of inches.
author: Shreyas Doshi
commentary: This concept has extremely powerful implications in how you approach product and even your personal life. We usually end up optimizing for maximum ROI leading to a tendency to pursue incremental options. Minimizing opportunity cost is a much better alternative.
summary: Shift from maximizing ROI (which favors cheap low-value tasks) to minimizing opportunity cost against the optimal option.
when_to_use: When in a high-leverage role and you want to stop chasing cheap incremental wins and pursue the highest-value alternative.
when_not_to_use: When work is low-leverage and execution-bound, where maximizing quick ROI wins is perfectly adequate.

name: AI/ML Model Metrics
body_md:
- Regression models
	- Common metrics : mean squared error, mean absolute error, or mean absolute percent error
	- If you want to train a model to reduce large outlier errors, choose MSE. If interpretability is more important and outlier errors are not a concern, choose MAE.
- Classification Models
	- Common Metrics : Precision, Recall, F1 Score
	- **Precision** is the ability of a classification model to identify only the relevant data points (Correct Predictions / Total Predictions)
	- **Recall** answers the question of how good is a model at identifying actual occurrences of objects in the data (Correct Predictions / Actual Occurrences) or what percent of the real occurrences were recalled by the model.
	- **F1 Score** combines precision and recall to produce an overall performance measure for a model. It ranges from zero to one, with one indicating perfect precision and recall. Typically, an f1 score greater than 0.7 or 0.8 is considered decent
	- **Confusion Matrix** is a grid that displays all predicted labels relative to all true labels. When looking at this chart, the values across a row should add up to 100%, while the values in the columns have no limitations. Confusion Matrix helps us identify where model biases may occur, and which classes we should focus on for future training cycles.
	- **Precision Recall Trade-off**: In some cases, we prioritize either recall or precision over the other. For example, when detecting early signs of lung cancer from X-rays, a recall near 1.0 is desired to find all potential patients with the disease. Precision can be sacrificed, meaning some false positives are acceptable, if follow-up examination costs are low. For certain models, like predicting credit card defaulters for a bank, high precision is crucial. This ensures that customers who are incorrectly classified as defaulters and denied a credit card based on the model's prediction are not lost.
summary: Common ML evaluation metrics (MSE/MAE, precision, recall, F1, confusion matrix) and the precision-recall trade-off.
when_to_use: When choosing how to evaluate an AI/ML model and weighing the precision-recall trade-off for regression or classification tasks.
when_not_to_use: When the question is product success metrics or business KPIs rather than model performance evaluation.

name: AI Vs. Machine Learning Vs. Data Science
body_md:
- **Artificial Intelligence (AI)**
	- **Definition:** AI is a broad field of computer science that focuses on creating intelligent machines or systems that can mimic human-like thinking and decision-making. It encompasses various techniques and technologies to enable machines to perform tasks that typically require human intelligence.
	- **Scope:** AI encompasses a wide range of areas, including natural language processing, computer vision, robotics, expert systems, and more. It seeks to develop systems that can reason, solve problems, understand and generate human language, and adapt to changing environments.
	- **Goal:** The primary goal of AI is to create machines that can perform tasks autonomously, adapt to new situations, learn from experience, and exhibit a form of intelligence.
- **Machine Learning (ML)**
	- **Definition:** ML is a subset of AI that focuses on the development of algorithms and statistical models that enable computers to learn and improve their performance on specific tasks through experience (data). ML algorithms aim to identify patterns and make data-driven predictions or decisions. There are other intelligence algorithms such as search algorithms for example that are used in artificial intelligence but they do not always use machine learning.
	- **Scope:** ML includes a variety of techniques and algorithms, such as supervised learning, unsupervised learning, reinforcement learning, and deep learning. It is widely used in applications like image recognition, natural language processing, recommendation systems, and anomaly detection.
	- **Goal:** The primary goal of ML is to enable machines to learn from data, generalize from that learning, and make predictions or decisions without being explicitly programmed for each task.
- **Data Science**
	- **Definition:** Data Science is an interdisciplinary field that combines elements of statistics, computer science, and domain expertise to extract insights and knowledge from data. It encompasses the entire data lifecycle, including data collection, cleaning, analysis, and interpretation.
	- **Scope:** Data Science involves tasks like data preprocessing, exploratory data analysis, statistical modeling, machine learning, data visualization, and communication of results. It often requires programming skills, knowledge of databases, and domain-specific expertise.
	- **Goal:** The primary goal of Data Science is to uncover valuable insights, patterns, and trends in data that can inform business decisions, solve complex problems, optimize processes, and provide actionable recommendations.
summary: Clarifies how AI, machine learning, and data science differ in scope, definition, and goals.
when_to_use: When you need to disambiguate AI, ML, and data science as nested-but-distinct fields before scoping an AI/data initiative or talking to specialists.
when_not_to_use: When you need concrete model-selection or evaluation guidance rather than conceptual boundaries between the disciplines.

name: API Architectures and Protocols
body_md:
- **Application Programming Interface** (API), is a **set of rules and protocols** that allows different software applications to communicate with each other. It defines the methods and data formats that developers can use to request and exchange information between different systems, services, or components.
- The data sent back as a response by the APIs is often called as **payload**
- There are several different API architectures and styles, each with its own characteristics and use cases. Some of the most common API architectures are explained below.
- **REST (Representational State Transfer):**
	- **Overview:** REST APIs are lightweight, scalable, and the most popular API architecture used by developers. It relies on a stateless client-server communication model and uses standard HTTP methods (Common HTTP methods - GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) for operations for a URL
	- **Key Features:**
		- **Client-Server Separation:** All client-server interactions must be in the form of a request from the client, followed by a response from the server. Servers can’t request and clients can’t respond.
		- **Uniform Interface:** All requests and responses must use HTTP as the communication protocol and be formatted in a specific way to ensure compatibility between any client and any server. Server responses are usually formatted in JavaScript Object Notation (JSON).
		- **Stateless:** Each client-server interaction is independent of every other interaction. The server stores no data from client requests and remembers nothing from past interactions.
		- **Layered system:** Requests and responses must always be formatted the same way, even when passed through intermediate servers between the client and the API.
		- **Cacheable:** Server responses should indicate whether a provided resource can be cached by the client and for how long.
	- **Example:**
		- GET /api/products - Retrieve a list of products.
		- POST /api/products - Create a new product.
		- PUT /api/products/1 - Update product with ID 1.
		- DELETE /api/products/1 - Delete product with ID 1.
- **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol):**
	- **Overview:** SOAP is a protocol for exchanging structured information in the implementation of web services. It uses XML as the message format and can be transported over various protocols, including HTTP, SMTP, and more.
	- **Key Features:**
		- Strict Specification: SOAP has a strict and well-defined specification, making it suitable for enterprise-level applications.
		- Extensibility: It supports complex data types and can work with different transport protocols.
	- **Example:**
		- A SOAP request might involve sending an XML message to a web service with an operation like "GetCustomerInfo."
- **GraphQL:**
	- **Overview:** GraphQL is a query language and runtime for APIs. Unlike REST, where clients often receive a fixed set of data, GraphQL allows clients to request precisely the data they need.
	- **Key Features:**
		- Flexible Queries: Clients define their data requirements through GraphQL queries, reducing over-fetching or under-fetching of data.
		- Strongly Typed: GraphQL schemas are strongly typed, and introspection allows clients to discover available types and operations.
	- **Example:**
		- Query: **`{ user(id: 1) { name email } }`**
- **gRPC:**
	- **Overview:** gRPC is a high-performance, open-source RPC (Remote Procedure Call) framework developed by Google. It uses Protocol Buffers for defining service contracts and supports multiple programming languages.
	- **Key Features:**
		- Efficient Communication: gRPC uses HTTP/2 for transport, offering features like multiplexing and binary serialization for efficient communication.
		- Code Generation: gRPC generates client and server code from service definitions, making it easier to maintain.
	- **Example:**
		- Define a service and its methods in a **`.proto`** file, then generate code to implement and call these methods in various programming languages.
- An **API contract** is a document that outlines the rules, requirements, and expectations of the API. The key components of an API contract can vary depending on the specific API and the needs of the users, but generally include the following:
	1. Endpoints: This refers to the specific URLs that can be called to access the API.
	2. Operations: This refers to the specific actions that can be performed on the API, such as GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE.
	3. Parameters: This refers to the data that can be passed to the API, such as query parameters, headers, and request bodies.
	4. Responses: This refers to the data that will be returned by the API, including status codes and response bodies.
	5. Authentication and authorization: This refers to the methods used to authenticate and authorize users to access the API.
	6. Error handling: This refers to the ways in which errors and exceptions are handled by the API.
	7. Usage restrictions: This refers to any limitations or restrictions on how the API can be used, such as rate limits or usage quotas.
commentary:
I have personally found it very effective to be closely involved in defining API contracts and the data payload. In most orgs, the backend team handles the business logic and API development, while multiple frontend teams handle the consumer-facing part. API contracts serve as an agreement between the backend and frontend teams on how to retrieve and update information.

I often see dev teams propose contracts that are closely tied to a specific solution. However, modifications or experiments often require changes to the payload structure, which can result in additional development and testing efforts. I personally feel that PMs should take the time to validate whether the proposed API contracts are future-proof and not closely linked to a specific solution.
summary: Compares REST, SOAP, GraphQL, and gRPC API styles and outlines what an API contract should specify.
when_to_use: When choosing or reviewing an API style (REST, SOAP, GraphQL, gRPC) and validating that the API contract is future-proof rather than tied to one solution.
when_not_to_use: When the concern is network transport protocols or front-end UX rather than service-to-service interface design.

name: Database Types
body_md:
- There are multiple ways to categorize different types of databases as follows:
	- Type of Usage - User Facing, Analytical and Operational
	- Technology and Features - SQL, NoSQL (many types) and Time Series
- **Relational Databases (RDBMS)**:
	- Structure: Data is organized into tables with rows and columns with a predefined format (schema)
	- Key Features: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) compliance, strong data consistency, and support for complex queries.
	- Use Cases: Suitable for structured data and applications with complex transactions like financial systems and CRM.
	- Examples: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server
- **NoSQL Databases**:
	- Structure: Data is stored in various formats, such as key-value, document, column-family, or graph.
	- Key Features: Scalability, flexible schema, high performance, and the ability to handle unstructured or semi-structured data.
	- Use Cases: Big data, real-time analytics, content management, and applications requiring rapid development.
	- Examples: MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis
- **Columnar Databases**:
	- Structure: Data is stored in columns rather than rows, making them suitable for analytical queries and data warehousing.
	- Key Features: High performance for analytical workloads, compression, and parallel processing.
	- Use Cases: Data analytics, business intelligence, and reporting.
	- Examples: Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery
- **Graph Databases**:
	- Structure: Data is organized in nodes and edges to represent relationships between entities.
	- Key Features: Efficient for querying and analyzing complex, interconnected data, ideal for recommendation engines and social networks.
	- Use Cases: Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs.
	- Examples: Neo4j, Amazon Neptune
- **In-Memory Databases**:
	- Structure: Data is stored in memory for faster access.
	- Key Features: Extremely fast data retrieval and low-latency performance.
	- Use Cases: Caching, session management, and real-time analytics.
	- Examples: Redis, Memcached
- **Time-Series Databases**:
	- Structure: Designed for time-ordered data, ideal for storing and querying time-series data.
	- Key Features: Optimized for high write and query performance of time-series data.
	- Use Cases: IoT applications, monitoring, and performance analysis.
	- Examples: InfluxDB, Prometheus
- **Document Databases**:
	- Structure: Data is stored as semi-structured documents, typically in JSON format.
	- Key Features: Flexible schema, good for content management and handling variable data structures.
	- Use Cases: Content management systems, catalogs, and catalogs.
	- Examples: MongoDB, CouchDB
- **Object Databases**:
	- Structure: Designed to store and manipulate objects directly, making it suitable for object-oriented applications.
	- Key Features: Direct mapping of objects to the database, making it efficient for object-oriented systems.
	- Use Cases: Object-oriented applications, simulations, and CAD software.
	- Examples: db4o, ObjectDB
- Some factors to consider while selecting a database:
	- **Scalability** - The system's ability to handle growth is important. There are two ways to scale: vertical and horizontal. Vertical scaling adds resources to a single server, while horizontal scaling adds more servers to the system.
	- **Performance** - Consider query efficiency and the balance between read and write performance. Different databases prioritize either read or write performance. Knowing your project's performance requirements will help you choose the right database type.
	- **Data Consistency** - It ensures that the information in the database remains accurate and up-to-date. Consider the importance of consistency for your product and how it might impact the user experience. For some applications, such as financial transactions, strong consistency is essential to ensure data integrity and avoid errors. In contrast, for other applications, like social media feeds or search indexes, eventual consistency may be sufficient, as temporary inconsistencies are less likely to negatively impact the user experience.
	- **Data Model** - It defines how data is structured, stored, and queried. Factors such as schema flexibility and support for complex data relationships should be taken into account when evaluating a database's data model. Relational databases have a fixed schema that ensures data integrity but can be limiting when dealing with diverse or rapidly changing data. NoSQL databases, on the other hand, offer more flexible or even schemaless data storage, which is advantageous for working with diverse or dynamic data.
	- **Security** - Protecting sensitive data from unauthorized access and attacks is vital. Key aspects to evaluate in a database's security features are authentication, authorization, encryption, and data protection.
	- **Cost** - licensing and hosting fees, as well as maintenance and management costs.
	- **Community and Ecosystem** - A strong community can provide valuable resources, such as documentation, tutorials, and forums, while a robust ecosystem can offer integration with other technologies, tools, and services.
summary: Surveys relational, NoSQL, columnar, graph, in-memory, time-series, document, and object databases plus selection factors.
when_to_use: When selecting a database type for a workload by weighing scalability, consistency, data model, performance, security, and cost.
when_not_to_use: When the data model and storage are fixed and the question is query tuning or schema detail rather than database-category choice.

name: Internet Protocols
body_md:
- Internet Protocols are a set of rules that governs the communication and exchange of data over the internet. Both the sender and receiver should follow the same protocols in order to communicate the data. Some popular internet protocols are explained below.
- **Internet Protocol (IP)** - This is the method or protocol by which data is sent from one computer to another on the internet. Each computer on the internet has at least one IP address that uniquely identifies it from all other computers on the internet. This address is just a string of numbers written in a certain format.
- **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** - TCP specifies how data is exchanged over the internet and how it should be broken into IP packets. This protocol enables the flow of data across IP address connections. It ensures that data is transmitted reliably and without errors
- **Domain Name System (DNS) -** The Domain Name System (DNS) protocol is used to translate human-readable domain names into machine-readable IP addresses. When a user types a domain name into a web browser such as google.com, the DNS client issues a DNS request or DNS address lookup to get IP address of server hosting the website.
- **User Datagram Protocol (UDP)** - It is a communications protocol that is primarily used to establish low-latency and loss-tolerating connections between applications on the internet. Unlike TCP, UDP is an unreliable and connectionless protocol, so there is no need to establish a connection prior to data transfer. It is typically used for real-time applications such as streaming video and audio, online gaming, and VoIP (Voice over Internet Protocol) where a small amount of lost data is acceptable and low latency is important.
- **Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** - This is the specification that enables the modern web. HTTP is an application layer protocol that allows the browser and server to communicate. Whenever a user opens their web browser, the user will indirectly use HTTP as this is the protocol that is being used to share text, images, and other multimedia files on the World Wide Web. **HTTPS** is an extension of the Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP). It is used for secure communication over a computer network with the **SSL/TLS** protocol for encryption and authentication
- **Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP) -** This protocol is used for sending and receiving email messages
- **File Transfer Protocol (FTP)** - This protocol is used for transferring files from one system to the other.
summary: Reference to the core internet protocols (IP, TCP, UDP, DNS, HTTP/HTTPS, SMTP, FTP) and what each one does.
when_to_use: When you need a working mental model of the core protocols (IP, TCP, UDP, DNS, HTTP/HTTPS, SMTP, FTP) that govern how data moves across the internet.
when_not_to_use: When the question is about application-layer API design or business logic rather than network transport fundamentals.

name: Mobile Apps - Native Vs. Mobile Web Vs. Hybrid
body_md:
- Native and hybrid apps are installed in an app store, whereas web apps are mobile-optimized webpages that look like an app. Both hybrid and web apps render HTML web pages, but hybrid apps use app-embedded browsers to do that.
- **Native apps** are installed on the device and accessed through icons on the home screen.
	- They are developed specifically for one platform and fully utilize device features such as the camera, GPS, accelerometer, compass, contact list, etc.
	- Native apps incorporate standard or app-defined gestures.
	- They can use the device's notification system and work offline.
	- Native apps are downloaded from app stores like Google Play or Apple's App Store.
- **Mobile web apps** are websites that resemble native applications but are not implemented as such.
	- They are run by a browser and typically written in HTML5.
	- Users can access them like any web page and create a bookmark on their home screen.
	- **Progressive Web App (PWA)** uses progressive enhancement for a reliable and integrated experience and can be installed.
- **Hybrid apps** combine native and web app features.
	- They reside in app stores and utilize device features.
	- Hybrid apps rely on HTML rendered in a browser embedded within the app.
	- They are often created as wrappers for existing web pages to have a presence in app stores.
	- Hybrid apps enable cross-platform development, reducing costs by reusing HTML code components on different mobile operating systems.
summary: Compares native, mobile-web/PWA, and hybrid mobile app approaches across device access, offline support, and cost.
aliases: Native vs hybrid vs web apps
when_to_use: When deciding how to build a mobile experience by weighing native vs mobile web vs hybrid against device access, offline needs, and cross-platform cost.
when_not_to_use: When the product is desktop/web-only or the trade-off is feature scope rather than mobile delivery approach.

name: Software Testing
body_md:
- The diagram below gives a high level overview of different types of software testing
- Functional Testing
	- **Unit Testing** is done on an individual unit or component to test its corrections. Typically, Unit testing is done by the developer at the application development phase.
	- **Integration Testing** is where two or more modules of an application are logically grouped together and tested as a whole. The focus of this type of testing is to find the defect on interface, communication, and data flow among modules.
	- **System Testing** is where tester evaluates the whole system against the specified requirements. There are multiple types of system testing such as end to end testing, smoke testing, happy path testing etc.
	- **Acceptance Testing** is where client/business/customer test the software with real time business scenarios. This is the last phase of testing, after which the software goes into production. This is also called User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
- Non Functional Testing
	- **Security Testing** is done to check how the software, application, or website is secure from internal and/or external threats. This testing includes how much software is secure from malicious programs, viruses and how secure & strong the authorization and authentication processes are
	- **Performance Testing** is testing of an application’s stability and response time by applying load
	- **Usability Testing** is testing an application from the user’s perspective to check the look and feel and user-friendliness.
	- **Compatibility testing** is testing which validates how software behaves and runs in a different environment, web servers, hardware, and network environment.
summary: Overview of software testing types across functional (unit, integration, system, acceptance) and non-functional categories.
when_to_use: When planning a test strategy and you need the landscape of functional (unit, integration, system, acceptance) and non-functional (security, performance, usability, compatibility) testing types.
when_not_to_use: When you need a specific test plan or tooling choice rather than an overview of testing categories.
